description: Figure 4 presents EBSD-based KAM maps overlaid on IQ maps for an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C low-density duplex steel, comparing air-cooled (AC, panels a–c) and furnace-cooled (FC, panels d–f) samples annealed at 800, 900, and 1000 °C. Red lines denote low-angle grain boundaries (>2°) and black lines denote high-angle grain boundaries (>15°), with a 50 μm scale bar in each panel. Visually, the AC samples exhibit needle-like α-ferrite embedded within austenite bands and dense low-angle grain boundaries in δ-ferrite that progressively diminish with increasing annealing temperature. The caption and text report corresponding low-angle boundary lengths of 4351.1 μm, 2685.4 μm, and 1634.3 μm, and average austenite grain sizes of 3.5 μm, 4.2 μm, and 5.8 μm for AC-A800, AC-A900, and AC-A1000, respectively. In contrast, the FC samples retain a pronounced banded morphology of δ-ferrite and prior austenite without needle-like α-ferrite, accompanied by darker IQ contrast indicating higher lattice distortion; the text reports coarser austenite grain sizes of 9.3 μm, 8.1 μm, and 10.1 μm. These microstructural differences confirm that air cooling produces a refined, recovered duplex structure with stabilized austenite, whereas furnace cooling yields banded coarse grains containing martensite, which governs the observed divergence in strength-ductility performance.
